00058|  Introduction to Electronics and Communication Engineering
00059| 
00060| Module 3
00061| 
00062| Introduction
00063| The Operational Amplifier (OP-AMP) is a key building block in analog integrated circuit design. An
00064| OP-AMP is composed by several transistors, passive elements (resistors and capacitors) and arranged such
00065| that its low frequency voltage gain is very high; the dc gain of the OP-AMP-741 is around
00066| 
00067| V/V (10 uV
00068| 
00069| at the input give us 1V at the output).
00070| The behavior of Op-Amp can be controlled by adding suitable feedback. They were mainly used to
00071| perform mathematical operations in analog computers before the invention of digital computer applications.
00072| The various practical applications of Op-Amp are Comparator, Precision rectifier, Instrumentation amplifier,
00073| Digital to analog converter etc.
00074| An Operational Amplifier is defined as: direct coupled, multistage, very high gain differential amplifier
00075| with high input impedance and low output impedance.
00076| Note:
00077| 
00078| 1. The output of an OP-AMP is proportional to the difference between the two input signals hence it is
00079| named as differential amplifier.
00080| 2. The OP-AMP is sometimes called as linear Integrated Circuit as the output signal is proportional to
00081| 
00082| the input signal (till the output reaches saturation level).
00083| 
00084| 3. The OP-AMP is a voltage controlled voltage source with very high gain.
00085| 
00086| Basics of OP-AMP
00087| 3.2.1 Symbol of OP-AMP
00088| The circuit symbol of an OP-AMP is shown in figure 3.1.The power supply voltages +VCC and –VEE,
00089| power the operational amplifier up and in general define the output voltage range of the amplifier. The terminals
00090| labelled with the “+” and the “-” signs are called non-inverting and inverting respectively.
00091| 
00092| Figure 3.1 Symbol of Op-Amp

00094|

00101| Equivalent Circuit of OP-AMP
00102| The equivalent circuit of an op-amp consists of an input resistance (𝑅𝑖) between the inverting and noninverting terminals, a voltage-controlled voltage source (𝐴 × 𝑉 ) that amplifies the differential input voltage
00103| 𝑉 = 𝑉 − 𝑉 and an output resistance (𝑅 ) in series with the dependent voltage source. This circuit is used
00104| for analysing op-amp behaviour by representing its characteristics with these physical components.
00105| The equivalent circuit of an Op-Amp is shown in figure 3.2(a).
00106| 
00107| Pin Configuration of Op-Amp
00108| The pin configuration of OP-AMP (IC741) is shown in Figure 3.3.
00109| 
00110| Figure 3.3

00120| Basic operation of Op-Amp
00121| 
00122| Figure 3.4
00123| 
00124| The graph that relates the output voltage to the input voltage is called the voltage transfer curve(VTC).
00125| The VTC of OP-AMP is shown in figure 3.4 and is fundamental in designing and understanding amplifier
00126| circuits. Note the two distinct regions of operation: one around V i =0V, the linear region where the
00127| output changes linearly with respect to input, and the other at which changes in V i has little effect on
00128| V o , the saturation region (non-linear behavior).
00129| Circuits with operational amplifiers can be designed to operate in both of these regions. In the linear region
00130| the slope of the line relating V o to Vi
00131| 
00132| is very large, indeed it is equal to the open loop gain A. For a 741
00133| 
00134| Op-Amp powered with V C C = +10V and V E E
00135| 
00136| = -10V, will saturate (reach the maximum output
00137| 
00138| voltage range) at about ±10 V. With an A=200,000V/V saturation occurs with an input differential
00139| voltage of 10/200,000 = 50μV, a very small voltage.
00140|

00155| Input stage of Op-Amp
00156| The basic requirements of the input stage of Op-Amp are
00157| i.
00158| ii.
00159| iii.
00160| iv.
00161| v.
00162| vi.
00163| vii.
00164| 
00165| high voltage gain
00166| high input impedance
00167| two input terminals
00168| small input offset voltage
00169| small input offset current
00170| high CMRR
00171| low input bias current
00172| 
00173| The dual input, input balanced output differential amplifier satisfies all these requirements and hence
00174| commonly used as the input stage. It provides the two input terminals, inverting and non-inverting.
00175| Intermediate stage
00176| The output of the input stage drives the next stage which is an intermediate stage. This is another
00177| differential amplifier with dual input, unbalanced i.e., single ended output. The overall gain requirement
00178| of the Op-amp is very high. The input stage alone cannot provide such a high gain. The main function of
00179| the intermediate stage is to provide an additional voltage gain required. Practically, the intermediate stage
00180| is not a single amplifier but a chain of cascaded amplifiers called as multistage amplifiers.
00181| Level Shifting Stage
00182| All the stages are directly coupled to each other. As the Op-Amp amplifies dc signals also, the
00183| coupling capacitors are not used to cascade the stages. Hence the dc quiescent voltage level of previous
00184| stage gets applied as the input to the next stage. Hence stage by stage dc level increases well above
00185| ground potential. Such a high dc voltage level may drive the transistors into saturation. This further may
00186| cause distortion in the output due to clipping. This may limit the maximum ac output swing without any
00187| 
00188| DEPT OF ELECTRONICS AND COMMUNICATION ENGG, DSCE
00189| 
00190| 5
00191| 
00192|  Introduction to Electronics and Communication Engineering
00193| 
00194| Module 3
00195| 
00196| distortion. Hence before the output sage, it is necessary to bring such a high dc voltage level to zero volts
00197| with respect to ground.
00198| Level shifter stage brings the dc level down to ground potential, when no signal is applied at the input
00199| terminals. Then the signal is given to the last stage which is the output stage.
00200| Output stage
00201| The output stage supplies the load and provides low output impedance. The requirements of good output
00202| stage are:
00203| Large output voltage swing capability
00204| Large output current swing capability
00205| Low output impedance
00206| Low quiescent power dissipation
00207| Short circuit protection
00208| A push-pull amplifier in class AB or class B operation satisfies all the above requirements hence
00209| commonly used in the output stage of an Op-Amp. The push-pull complementary amplifier stage increase
00210| the output voltage swing and keeps the voltage swing symmetrical with respect to ground. The stage
00211| raises the current supplying capability of the Op-Amp.
00212|

00221| Characteristics of Op-Amp
00222| Ideal Characteristics of Op-Amp
00223| An ideal Op-Amp has the following characteristics.
00224| 1. Infinite open loop voltage gain ( i.e., A =∞)
00225| 2. Infinite input impedance (
00226| 
00227| = ∞): An ideal Op-Amp does not draw any current from voltage
00228| 
00229| sources connected to its input terminals.
00230| 3. Zero output impedance (Ro =0): The output impedance is independent of the current drawn from
00231| it.
00232| 
00233| Figure 3.5
00234| 
00235| 4. Infinite Bandwidth (B.W. = ∞): Amplifies signals of any frequency ranges with constant gain.
00236| 5. Infinite Common mode rejection ratio (i.e., CMRR =∞) the ratio of differential voltage gain to
00237| common-mode voltage gain.
00238| 
00239| CMRR= Ad/Ac
00240| 6. Infinite slew rate (i.e., S=∞); the rate of change of output voltage per unit time.
00241| S=
00242| 7. Zero power supply rejection ratio (PSRR = 0); output voltage is zero when power supply
00243| VCC =0.
00244| 8. Zero offset voltage: when the input voltages are zero, the output voltage will also be
00245| zero.
00246| 9. Perfect balance: the output voltage is zero when the input voltages at the two input

00257| Characteristics of practical Op-Amp

00268| Op-Amp parameters
00269| Slew rate
00270| Slew rate of an Op-amp is defined as the maximum time rate of change of its output voltage, expressed in
00271| volt per microsecond.
00272| ….. V/µs
00273| Slew rate is a measure of how fast the op-amp output can change in response to changes in the input
00274| signal. This limits the maximum operating frequency of op-amp. Slew rate depends on factors such as
00275| load capacitance. Typical rate for µA741 is 0.5V/µs.
00276|

00316| Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)
00317| The usefulness of the op amp is due to its ability to amplify differential signals. In fact the ideal op amp
00318| has an infinite gain for differential voltage signals. It is desired that signals that are common to both
00319| inputs “Common Mode (CM) Signals” be rejected by the amplifier (causing zero output voltage). An
00320| ideal op amp has the ability to completely reject those CM signals; thus having infinite Common Mode
00321| Rejection (CMR) ability. But unfortunately a small voltage appears for common mode signals in practical
00322| Op-Amp. This is caused by inevitable small differences in gain between the inverting and the noninverting inputs. The op amp therefore has some gain to common mode signals, which is indicated by the
00323| parameter Common Mode Voltage Gain (
00324| mode gain (
00325| 
00326| ). The ratio of differential mode gain (
00327| 
00328| ) to the common
00329| 
00330| ) is termed as CMRR. It is a figure merit or performance measure of Op-Amp. Since
00331| 
00332| CMRR is large number, sometimes it is expressed in dB.
00333| CMRR =
00334| CMRR = 20 ...........................dB
00335| Input offset voltage (Vos) : It is the differential input required to make the output voltage zero (typically
00336| 1mV)
00337| Output offset voltage: It is the output voltage obtained when both the inputs are grounded.
00338| Input bias current (IB) : It is the average of separate currents entering the two input
00339| terminals of balanced amplifier and given as IB = (IB1 + IB2 ) / 2 with Vo = 0V.
00340| Input offset current (Iio): It is the difference between the two input currents entering the two
00341| terminals of a balanced amplifier.
00342| Iio =(IB1 - IB2 ) with Vo = 0V
00343| Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR) : It is the measure of ability of op-amp to disregard the changes
00344| in power supply voltage

00390| 3.3.4 Negative feedback concept
00391| 
00392| Any input signal slightly greater than zero drive the output to saturation level because of very high gain. So,
00393| when operated in open-loop, the output of the OPAMP is either negative or positive saturation or switches
00394| between positive and negative saturation levels (comparator). Therefore, open loop op-amp is not used in
00395| linear applications. With negative feedback, the voltage gain (
00396| 
00397| ) can be reduced and controlled so that op-
00398| 
00399| amp can function as a linear amplifier.
00400| Also, more importantly, for an amplifier the product of gain and bandwidth called the ‘Gain-Bandwidth
00401| product’ is constant. An Op-amp with very large gain and small bandwidth is of less use. So, a negative
00402| feedback path automatically reduces the gain while increasing the bandwidth.
00403| Advantages of negative feedback
00404| •
00405| 
00406| Amplifier becomes more linear
00407| 
00408| •
00409| 
00410| Input and output impedances are modified to their ideal values
00411| 
00412| •
00413| 
00414| Gain of the amplifier becomes more stable
00415| 
00416| •
00417| 
00418| Bandwidth of amplifier increases
00419|
